text: CABANNES (Les), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ariège, arrond. of Foix.—The cant., comprising 25 communes, had a pop. of 8,219 in 1831.—The com. is on the l. bank of the Ariege, 15 m. SSE of Foix. Pop. 668.
CABANS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Cadouin. Pop 1,154.

CABAPUANA, or CAMAPOAN, a river of Brazil, rising in the Serra do Pico, near the source of the Muriaré, and flowing E to the Atlantic, which it enters in S lat. 21° 25'. Throughout 15 leagues of its course, it separates the provinces of Rio-de-Janeiro and Espirito-Sancto.
CABARDA. See KABARDAH.
CABARRUBIAS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 21 m. SSE of Burgos, on the r. bank of the Arlanza. Pop. 900.

CABARRUS, a county in the S part of the state of N Carolina, U. S., watered by the Rocky river, a branch of the Yadkin. Area 300 sq. m. The chief produce is cotton. Pop. 9,259. The cap. is Concord, 139 m. WSW of Raleigh.
CABASES, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 30 m. NW of Tarragona, at the foot of the Sierra-dela-Llena.
CABASSE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Var, cant. of Basse, on the Issolle, 8 m. ENE of Brignolles. Pop. 1,448.
CAB-BELULL, a station in the desert between Kordofan and Darfur, two days from Caccie, at which, it is reported, there exist extensive ruins, apparently in the Egyptian style of architecture.
CABE, a river of Spain, which rises near San Juan in Galicia, passes Monforte, and flows into the Sil, on the r. bank, after a course of 54 m. from NE to SW.
CABEÇAO, or Cabeco-de-Vide, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alentejo, 12 m. SW of Aviz. Pop. 1,200.
CABECEIRA-DO-RIO-PRETO, a military post CAB 144 on the Rio Preto, a branch of the Parahibuna, in the Brazilian prov. of Minas-Geraes.
CABEÇO-DE-MARIA, a summit of the Sierra d'Alcaraz, in Spain, in the prov. of La Mancha. Alt. 6,861 ft. above sea-level.
CABEÇON. See CABEZON.
CABEDELLO, a fort of Brazil, on the Parahiba river, in the prov. of Parahiba, in S lat. 6° 57', 2 m. from the sea.
CABELL, a county in the W part of the state of Virginia, U. S., on the Ohio, and watered by Guyandott river. Area about 1,000 sq. m. Pop. 8,163. The cap. is Barboursville.

CABELLO (Puerto), a port of Venezuela, on the Caribbean sea, in N lat. 10° 29' 45", W long. 68° 7' 35", 9 m. W of Turiamo, and 25 m. E of Point Tucacas. The town is chiefly built on an island, or rather an insulated piece of ground; and is reported to be exceedingly unhealthy. Pop. from 7,000 to 8,000. The entrance to the harbour is formed by several islets and tongues of low land covered with mangroves. This channel opens into a capacious bay, affording excellent and well-sheltered anchorage. In this harbour all Spanish merchant-ships trading with La Guayra are careened and winter; and there is always a body of good shipwrights here. Of 17,636,000 francs of imports into Venezuela in 1840, C. received 2,984,000; and of 23,876,000 francs
CABELLO-DA-VELHA, a bay on the coast of Brazil, in the prov. of Maranham, between Cape Cuma on the S, and Turivassu on the N, in S lat. 1° 40'. The river Curupuru falls into it.

CABENDA, a town on the coast of Angola in W Africa, in S lat. 5° 32', on a bay of the same name which affords good shelter for shipping. Adams describes the whole coast between this place and the river Loango Luiza, an extent of 9 leagues, as particularly healthy, and the natives as mild and inoffensive; but Degrandpre asserts that the natives of C. are ruder and more difficult to treat with than the other tribes on this coast. Captain Owen says the whole coast from C. to the Zahir, a distance of 36 m., is particularly fertile, and has a luxuriant appearance. C. is resorted to principally by slavers.

CABERABERA, a district and town of W Africa, in Benguela, dependent on Bihe, between the rivers Cubango and Cuvo.—Douville. CABES, GABES, or GABS, a town and port of Tunis, on the gulf of the same name. The town is situated on the Wad-er-rif river, nearly 1 m. from the sea, on the shore of which it once stood, in N lat. 33° 53', E long. 10° 4'. It is surrounded by several villages. Blaquiere says it contains 30,000 souls; and that it carries on a considerable trade with Tunis and Kairwan. A principal article of its commerce is hennah the Lawsonia inermis of botanists, which is extensively cultivated in its environs. On a rising ground, half-a-mile from the modern town, are some ruins which may mark the site of the ancient Epichus of Scylax, and Tacape of Ptolemy.

CABES (GULF OF), an indentation of the coast of Tunis, in N Africa, lying between Cape-Capudiah or Caput Vada, on the N, and Ras Shabbu on the S. These capes are nearly on the same meridian, and 120 m. distant from each other; and the depth of the gulf on the parallel of 34° 10' N is 70 m. The shores of the gulf from Cape Capudiah SW to Sfax or Sfakus, are of considerable height, and form the W side of a channel, of which the E side is formed by the islands of Kerkinah and Gharbi. These islands are very low and covered with date-trees. The channel betwixt them and the mainland is contracted by sandy shallows and rocky banks, and the tide rises and falls in it 5 ft., and runs 2½ m. an hour. From Sfax, in N lat. 34° 43', to opposite the Surkenis islets in N lat. 34° 20', the land rises considerably inland towards a chain of mountains called Jebel Tielth. From the Surkenis isles to Cabes, the coast is clear and safe, with from 7 to 8 fath. water at 4 m. from the shore. At Cabes the coast is low, and trends by SE to E by N to Point Zurshaf, nearly opposite the large island of Jerba, from which point it forms an elliptical bay opposite the S end of that island and then runs SE to Ras Shabbu. The gulf of C. is the Minor Syrtis of the ancients; but no longer presents those terrors to mariners with which it was clothed in former ages. The lotus tree still grows plentifully as of old all along its shores.
CABESTERRE, a general name by which the summits of the West India islands are distinguished.
CABEZA, a town of Spain, in Leon, subdivision and 30 m. WNW of Salamanca.

CABEZA, a town of Bolivia, in the intendancy and 100 m. S of Sta Cruz de la Sierra, on the l. bank of the Rio Grande or Guapai.—Also a village 52 m. SSE of Potosi, on an affluent of the Rio Grande branch of the Pilaya.

CABEZA DEL BUEY, a town of Spain in Estremadura, in the prov. and 75 m. ESE of Badajoz, and 20 m. ESE of Castuera. Pop. 5,994. It possesses some manufactories of cloth. Quintana, author of the tragedy of Pelayo, was a native of this place.
CABEZA DEL BUEY, a lake of La Plata, in the prov. of Buenos Ayres, 30 WNW of La Blanca Fte., in S lat. 36° 11', and W long. 61° 12'. 45° 15'.
CABEZA-DE-VACA, a well-marked point on the coast of Chili, 12 m. N of Port Caldera. The coast between forms several small bays with rocky points.
CABEZADO BOY, a town of Brazil, in the prov. and 150 m. SW of Bahia.
CABEZA MESSADA, a town of Spain, in New Castile, prov. and 50 m. ESE of Toledo, on the r. bank of the Rianzares.
CABEZARADOS, a town of Spain, in New Cas- 97° 31'. tile, prov. of La Mancha, 19 m. SW of Cuidad Real.
CABEZAS, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, prov. and 22 m. S of Seville, on a hill near a tributary of the Guadalquiver. Pop. 1,000.
CABEZAS (Los), a shoal in the Bahama Old channel, near the N coast of the island of Cuba, in N lat. 23° 13', and W long. 80° 42'.
CABEZO, a creek and village on the bay of Campeachy, in the gulf of Mexico, in N lat. 20° 1', and W long. 92° 15'.
CABEZON, a town of Spain, in Leon, subdivision and 7 m. NE of Valladolid, on the l. bank of the Pisuerga, in a district which has the reputation of producing the best wine in the province.
CABEZON DE LA SAL, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 25 m. WSW of Santander, and 10 m. SW of Santillana.

CABEZUELA, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, prov. and 65 m. NNE of Caceres, and 15 m. NE of Plasencia, on the l. bank of the Jerte. CABI, CUBBIE, or KABI, a district of Sudan, in the territory of Houssa, situated at the junction of the Guarama with the Niger. It was visited by Mungo Park, but is still nearly unknown to Europeans.
CABIA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 7 m. WSW of Burgos, on the l. bank of the Arcos, a little above the confluence of that river with the Arlanzon. Villa Rica.
CABIERES, a river of Bolivia, which takes its rise in the Allos de Intinuyo, runs N, and forms one of the tributaries of the Mamore.
CABILA, a town of Greece, in the prov. and 32 m. NE of Tricali, on the r. bank of the Saranta Poros, 5 m. WNW of Turnovo.
CABIN POINT, a village of Surrey co., in the state of Virginia, 43 m. SE of Richmond, near Chipoak creek, consisting of 18 or 20 dwellings.
CABINTEELY, a village in the parishes of Tully and Killiney, in co. Dublin, 6 m. SE by S of Dublin. Janeiro. Pop. 333.
CABIXI, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Mato- Grosso, rising in the Serra Parecis, and falling into the Guapore, on the r. bank, after a course of 60 m. 11. CAB
CABLE ISLAND, an islet belonging to co. Cork, 31 m. S by W of Youghal.
CABO. See CAPE.

CABO-DE-GATA (EL), or CUEVA DEL GATO, a remarkable cavern, in the side of a lofty mountain, 5 m. SW of Ronda, in the prov. of Granada in Spain. A stream which issues from it falls into the Guadiaro, on the r. bank.
CABO FRIO. See FRIO (CAPE).
CABO VERDE. See CAPE VERDE.
CABORCA, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Sonora, on the Rio de S. Ignacio, 155 m. W of Arispe.

CABOT, a township of Caledonia co., in the state of Vermont, U. S., 20 m. NE of Montpelier, drained by the head-streams of the Onion river and tributaries of the Connecticut. Its surface, which is hilly, is chiefly in pasture. Pop. in 1840, 1,440.
CABOT'S HEAD, a promontory of Upper Canada, at the NE extremity of the peninsula which separates Georgian bay from Lake Huron, in N lat.
CABOTVILLE, a village of Springfield township, Hampden co., in the state of Massachusetts, U.S., 95 m. SW of Boston, at the confluence of the Chickapee with the Connecticut.
CABOURN, a parish of Lincolnshire, 2 m. ENE of Caistor. Area 2,860 acres. Pop. in 1841, 166.
CABOZE, or Cabossa, an island of the Indian ocean, in the Mergui archipelago, to the N of the island of Tanasserim, in N lat. 12° 45', and E long.

CABRA, a town of Spain, in Aragon, prov. and 35 m. NNE of Teruel, and 4 m. SE of Montalban. -Also a town of Spain, in Andalusia, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 30 m. SSE of Cordova, on a river of the same name.—Also a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 28 m. SE of Jaen, to the E of the Jandulilla.
CABRA, a mountain in the island of Cuba, near the W extremity, 8 m. N of Cortes bay.

CABRA, a town of Sudan, in the kingdom and 5 m. S of Timbuctu, on the N bank of the Niger. It is situated on a small hill which protects it from the inundations of the river. The streets are narrow but neat; the houses are built of earth with terraced roofs. Pop. 1,000 or 1,200. The Tuaricks levy their duties on vessels at this place. A little tobacco and a few date trees are the only signs of vegetation in the vicinity; and the road to Timbuctu lies through deep loose sand.

CABRACH, a parish partly in Aberdeenshire, but chiefly in Banff, intersected by the Blackwater and the Deveron. Its surface, amounting to 80 sq. m., is altogether mountainous, or bleak pastoral upland. Pop. in 1801, 684; in 1841, 827, of whom 461 were in Banffshire.
CABRADILLA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 12 m. SW of Salamanca.
CABRAL, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas Geraes, near the l. bank of the Velhas, 200 m. N of
CABRALIA, a port of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, and 12 m. N of Porto Seguro, the only bay in the district which is accessible to large vessels.

CABRAS, a parish and large village of the island of Sardinia, prov. of Busachi, 3 m. WNW of Oristano, and 1 m. from the sea, on a lagune called the Mare-de-Pontis, which affords excellent fish, particularly eels and mullets. Pop. 2,800. The environs afford corn, wine, and dried fruits. - Also an islet off the coast of Parati, in the Brazilian prov. of Rio-de-
CABRELLA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, in the comarca and 30 m. ENE of Setubal, on the Marateca. It contains about 300 houses.

CABRERA, a river of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. of Villafranca, which issues from lake Bana, at the base of the Sierra de Porto, runs E to the village of Nogar, thence bends WNW, and after a total course of about 40 m., falls into the Sil, on the confines of Galicia.

CABRE'RA, the ancient Capraria, one of the Balearic group, in the Mediterranean, lying S of the island of Majorca, from which it is separated by a channel 9 m. in breadth, in N lat. 39° 7½, and E long. 2° 59'. It is 3½ m. in length, from NE to SW, and about 13 m. in breadth; and rises to a considerable height. The soil is scanty and almost entirely uncultivated: the industry of the inhabitants, who are now few in number, consisting chiefly in the rearing of goats,—the origin, it is supposed, of the name of the island. On the NE side is a commodious and well-sheltered port, defended by a strong castle. During the peninsular war the Spaniards used this island as a place of confinement for several thousand French prisoners.—Also one of the Ionian islands, lying off the SW extremity of the Morea, in N lat. 36° 40′, E long. 21° 47½′, 3 m. SE of Sapienza. It is about 4 m. in length from N to S, and 2 m. in breadth. Half-a-league from its NE end, Smyth found 106 fathoms.—Also an islet off the N coast of Sardinia, in N lat. 41° 16'.

CABRERA, a river of Spain, rising in Lake Barra, in the prov. of Villafranca, in Leon; running E to Nogar, where it abruptly turns W, and flows to the Sil, which it joins on the l. bank, after a course of 40 m.
CABRERA-DE-BUYTRAGO, a village of Spain, in the prov. of Madrid, 9 m. SE of Buytrago.
CABRERETS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Lot, cant. of Lauzes, on the Celle river, 12 m. ENE of Cahors. Pop. 959.
CABREROS-DEL-MONTE, a village of Spain, in the prov. and 30 m. WNW of Valladolid.
CABREROS-DEL-RIO, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 15 m. S of Leon, near the l. bank of the Esla.
CABRESPINE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aude, cant. of Peyriac-Minervois, on the Clamous. Pop. 871.
CABREZE, a river of SE Africa, in Monomotapa, which flows into the Manzora, an affluent of the Zambeze, after a course from S to N of 150 m.

CABRIEL, a river of Spain, which rises near the sources of the Tagus in the Sierra d'Albarracin, in the prov. of Teruel; flows S to Pesquera, where it turns SE; and joins the Jucar under the walls of Cofruentes, after a course of 140 m., in which it receives the Guadaçaon on the r. and the Moya on the l.
CABRIERES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, cant. of L'Isle, 18 m. ESE of Avignon. Pop. 775. Its inhabitants, accused of heresy, were massacred in the reign of Francis I.
CABRILLAS, a village of Spain, in the prov. and 30 m. SW of Salamanca, on a hill rising near the Yeltes.
CABRIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Var, cant. of Saint-Vallier, 2 m. W of Grasse. Pop. 1,846.
CABRITA, an islet off the N coast of Jamaica, at the entrance of Port Maria.

CABRON (CAPE), or Point a' Grapin, one of the two points of the peninsula of Samana, at the NE end of the island of Saint Domingo, in N lat. 19° 21' 30", W long. 69° 14' 13". Both points are bluff and steep, and about 4 or 5 m. asunder.

CABRUNA, an islet off the coast of Colombia, one of the San Bernardo cluster, 3 m. from Point San Bernardo, on the E side of the Salamanquilla channel. CAB Caraccas, on the N bank of the Orinoco, at the mouth of the Mancapra, 150 m. S by E of San Sebastian. . CABUÇU, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Riode-Janeiro, in the district of Itabourahi, which unites with the Aldea, an affluent of the Macacu.—Also a tributary of the Rio Guandu, in the same province. CABUL, CAUBUL [Elphingstone], KABUL [Burnes], a large and important prov. of Afghanistan, extending from the Hindukoosh on the N to some distance S of Ghuzni; and from Bamian or from the pass of Akrobat, on the W, to the Khyber mountains on the E. The countries under the sovereignty of the king of Cabul once extended 16 degrees in long. from Suhind, 150 m. from Delhi, to Meshid, about an equal distance from the Caspian sea; and in breadth 13 degrees of lat., from the Oxus to the Persian gulf. But the name no longer designates an independent state; and has given place in modern maps to the more general name of Afghanistan: which see.

CABUL, the second capital of Afghanistan, and before the dismemberment of the Durani empire, the seat of government, stands on the Cabul river, in N lat. 34° 30', E long. 69° 6', or according to some accounts, in 34° 10' N lat., and 69° 15' E long., at an alt. of 6,396 ft. above sea-level: 839 m. by road from Delhi; 232 m. from Candahar; and 1,815 m. from Calcutta. The houses are built of sun-dried bricks and wood, few of them more than two stories high. "Let the reader conceive a broken succession of houses, composed of mud walls of different elevations, pierced here and there with wooden pipes to carry off the rain from the flat roofs which it would otherwise injure; then let him imagine a few square low doors opening under the eaves of the first story, projecting over a sort of trottoir formed by the wearing away of the middle of a road so angular that no wheel-carriage could be driven along safely; now and then a larger door interposing, the entrance to the residence of some great man, with a mulberry tree occasionally peering over the wall; add to this a thick crowd, and he will form a good idea of a Cabul street." [Vigne.] The city is about 3 m. in circuit, and is commanded from eminences on the NW. The Balahissar, or fort, on the SE side of the city, is about half-a-mile long, and a quarter of a mile in breadth, and is surrounded by lofty walls, and a broad stagnant moat. The principal bazaar, or rather a succession of bazaars, forming a continuous street, presented a splendid appearance when Gerard visited C. in 1832; but these buildings have been demolished, or greatly injured, in the troublous scenes through which the city has lately passed. Vigne and Burnes concur in estimating the pop. of C. in 1836 at 60,000; made up of Afghans, Kuzzilbashes or Persians, Tajiks, Hindus, Armenians, Jews, and Christians. The hills around C. are bare and rocky; and the plain by nature is almost equally barren; but time and human industry have combined to form upon it a soil the fertility of which is rarely exceeded. The climate of C. is hot, considering its elevation. The temp. in the shade generally exceeds 80° in the month of August.—C. has few manufactures, but is the centre of a large internal traffic, and is or was a great entrepot of trade between India and Afghanistan and Turkistan. An article in the Calcutta Gazette of 1835 states, that although the anarchy reigning in Afghanistan for a long period, and the ambiguous political relations of the several petty governments at that time established in it, had not been favourable to the prosecution of its commerce, yet it would appear that during the last few years the trade of C. had considerably increased: the custom-house under the Suddozie princes being farmed for only 25,000 rupees per annum, and that of Ghuzni for only 7,000

CABRUTA, a town of Venezuela, in the prov. of rupees per annum, whereas the last year (1834) the former was farmed for one lac and 40,000 rupees, | and the latter for 80,000 rupees; while the duties levied were at the same rate, viz., 21 per cent. ad gota. valorem. With respect to the value of the trade of C .- the same article continues - it may be observed that there are six points within its territories where duties on merchandise are levied, viz., C., Ghuzni, Bamian, Charreekar, Loghur, and Jelalabad. The transit duties at these several places in 1834 p. 156. were farmed as follows:- Cabul. <math>1,40,900 \times 40 = 56,00,000 \div 12</math> rupees per £ sterling, £466,666 + Ghuzni. 266,666 + <math>80,000 \times 40 = 20,00,000 \div 12</math> ... Bamian. 166,666 + <math>40 = 20,00,000 \div 12</math> 50,000 Charreekar. 33,333 + <math>10,000 \times 40 = 4,00,000 \div 12</math> ... Loghur. 20,000 <math>6,000 \times 40 = 2,40,000 \div 12</math> Jelalabad. 40,000 <math>12,000 \times 40 = 4,80,000 \div 12</math> ... Value of (Total amount £993,331 2,98,000 merchandise. of duties. This table only shows the amount of benefit to the state derived from direct duties on merchandise, as duties are levied on the same goods frequently at two places, as at Ghuzni and Cabul, &c.; yet when it is considered that the farmers of them reap a profit, and that smuggling to a very great extent prevails, while there is constant evasion of payment of duty, the calculation that the trade of C. with her neighbours may be of the value of £1,000,000, is likely to fall short of, rather than to exceed, the truth. Of this sum £200,000 will be the value of its trade with Turkistan. It appears that the commercial transactions of C. with Russia were considerable at the period above alluded to, which might in part be attributed to the comparative facility and safety of the communications between C. and Bokhara. From Orenburg to Bokhara, there are 62 kaffa or camelmarches; and from Bokhara to C. 35 marches: being a total of 97, independent of halts. The chief articles imported from Russia are broad cloth, chintzes, silk goods, velvets, paper, steel and copper wire, iron in bars, and sugar.—This city is mentioned by Arabian historians, in the 8th cent., as the residence of a Hindu prince. In 977 it was captured by Subactagin, the founder of the Ghaznevide dynasty. Towards the close of the 14th cent. Tamerlane took possession of it; and his great-grandson, Baber, made it the seat of his government. In 1738 it was taken by Nadir Shah; and towards the close of last century Timur Shah made it the cap. of the Durani empire, which crumbled to pieces in 1809. In very recent times it was the scene of those deplorable events related in our historical notice of Afghanistan.

CABUL RIVER, or Jui Shir, a large river of Eastern Afghanistan, formed by the junction of two main streams at Khamma, and carrying the waters of the great valley of Cabul to the Indus. See AFGHANISTAN, p. 64. 6,394.
CABULLA PASS, a defile in New Granada, prov. of Bogota, between the ranges of the Chingasa and the Suma Paz, and 15 m. S of Santa Fe de Bogota.
CABULLARE, a river of Venezuela, which separates from the Apure at Apurito, inosculates with the Arauca, and flows into the Orinoco 8 m. SSW of the embouchure of the Apure.
CABULLARITO LAKE, an expansion of one of the arms of the Arauca, in Venezuela, 15 m. W of its confluence with the Orinoco, and 20 m. NNE of the mouth of the Arauca. CAC
CABULLARO, a town of New Granada, on the N bank of the Meta, 60 m. SW of Santa Fe de Bo-
CABUS, a township in the p. of Garstang, Lancashire, intersected by the Lancaster canal, and the Lancaster and Preston railway. Area 1,220 acres. Pop. in 1841, 253.
CABYLES, or KABYLES. See article ALGIERS,

CACA, or Mapiri, a river of Bolivia, in the prov. of Larecaja, which rises on the central ridge of the Cordillera, below Zarata, and flows in a prevailingly NNE course to the Beni, which it joins on the l. bank, after uniting with the Tipuani, the Challana and the Corvico, all descending from the eastern declivity of the same Cordillera.
CACAGNE, a town of Senegambia, on the l. bank of the Gambia, near its head, 220 m. NNE of Sierra Leone.
CACAHITLA, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of La Puebla, on the r. bank of the Yopez river, and about 100 m. from its mouth.

CACAHUAMILPA, a remarkable cave in a bleak and bare chain of mountains to the SE of the city of Mexico, near Cuautla Amilpas. Its entrance is by a superb portal 70 ft. high, and 150 ft. wide. The first sala, or hall, is 200 ft. long, 170 ft. wide, and 150 ft. in height. Its walls are shaded with green and orange; great sheets of stalactites hang from the roof; and a thousand fantastic forms surround it on all sides. To this succeeds a second hall, 400 ft. in length, and as lofty as the former. Then follow a double gallery supported by enormous pyramidal stalagmite formations, and a vast corridor on lofty pillars. The beauties and marvels of this "prodigious subterranean palace" were first and well-described in the lively letters entitled Life in Mexico [Lond., 1843, 8vo.].

CACAPA'BA, a district and small town of Brazil, in the prov. of San-Pedro-do-Rio-Grande, 62 m. SE of Rio-Pardo, and 100 m. ESE of Porto Alegre. The rivers Jacuhi and Camacuan respectively bound it on the N and S. Pop. of the district, chiefly Indian, 3,000. — Also a village in the prov. of San Paulo, 10 m. from Taubaté. Tobacco and millet are extensively grown in the environs; and pigs and poultry are reared for the markets of San Paulo and Rio-de-Janeiro.

CACAPON, a river in the state of Virginia, U.S., which takes its rise in the SE corner of Hardy co., and running N through Hampshire and Morgan counties, along the W side of the mountain ridge of the same name, falls into the Potomac 4 m. W of Bath, after a total course of 75 m.—Also a creek in Hampshire co., which unites with the Potomac 4 m. E of the junction of the S branch of that river.
CACARDISTA MOUNTAIN, a ridge in Albania, sanjak of Janina, forming the NE extremity of the chain which separates the basins of the Aspro Potamo and Mezzovo.
CACCAMO, a town of the island of Sicily, cap. of a cant., in the prov. and 23 m. SE of Palermo, district and near the r. bank of the Termini. Pop.

CACCIA CAPE, a headland of the W coast of the island of Sardinia, 12 m. W of Alghero, forming the S extremity of the Nurra mountains, in N lat. 40° 35', and E long. 8° 5'. It contains a curious grotto, incrusted with stalactites, called the grotto of Neptune.
CACCIORNA. See Andorno-Cacciarno.
CACELLA, a town and port of Portugal, in the prov. of Algarve, 8 m. E of Tavira, near the mouth of the Guadiana. Pop. 1,000.

CACERES, a province and town of Spain. The prov., forming the N part of Estremadura, is bounded on the N by the provinces of Salamanca and Arvila; on the E by Toledo and Ciudad-Real; on the S by Badajoz; and on the W by Portugal. Its length from N to S is about 100 m.; its mean breadth 92 m. The superficies is estimated at 615 Spanish sq. leagues. The sierras of Gredos and Gata rise on its N skirts; those of San Benito, San Pedro, and Guadaloupe in the S. The Tagus intersects it from E to W; and the Tietar, the Alagon, the Solor, and the Rio del Monte, are the next considerable streams. The soil is fertile. The pop. in 1834 was estimated at 241,328. It is divided into 13 partidos, which are subdivided into 227 pueblos. The partidos are: Alcantara, C., Coria, Garrovillas, Granilla, Hoyos, Jarandilla, Logrosan, Montauchez, Navalmoral-dela-Mata, Plasencia, Truxillo, and Valencia-de-Alcantara.

CACERES, the cap. of the above prov., is situated on an escarpment of the Sierra-de-Fuentes, at the foot of which flows a small river which takes its name from the town, an affluent of the Mayasco, 48 m. NNE of Badajoz. Its only remarkable building is a suppressed Jesuit convent, now an hospital, one of five convents in the town. It is the seat of the bishop of Coria. Pop. 9,521. It has some leather and woollen manufactures. Great numbers of swine are reared in the vicinity; and the town is noted for its fine Merino wool. It occupies the site of the Roman Castra Cacilia.
CACERES, a town of New Granada, in the prov. of Popayan, on a tributary of the Cauca, 180 m. SSW of Antioquia.
CACERES (NUEVA), a town on the E coast of the island of Lucon, in the Philippines, in the prov. of Camarinas, on the bay of San Miguel.

CACERUBU, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro; rising in the Sierra de Sambé; receiving the Bonito, Tangua, Muticapira, and Igua; and joining the Macacu, after a course of 30 m. CACHAO, CACHEU, or SANTO DOMINGO, a river on the coast of Senegambia, which enters the sea in 12° 5' N lat. It appears to inosculate with a branch of the Rio Grande.—About 17 m. above its mouth, on an island, is a small fort, formerly the principal Portuguese establishment in this quarter of Africa. This island, which is formed by the C. river and another called Jatt's river, is 45 m. in length, and is inhabited by Papels. It bears the name of the fort.
CACHAO. See KACHAO.

CACHAR, a province of Hindostan, bounded on the N by the Brahmaputra river, which separates it from Assam; on the E by Munipore and the Birmese territories; on the S by Sylhet; and on the W by Jyntea. It lies between the parallels of 24° and 27° N; and originally might contain an area of 9,000 sq. m. The surface is in general mountainous; and much of it is covered with jungle. The ancient name of this territory was Hairumbo, and its cap. Grobarge; 20 m. to the N of the modern cap. is Cospore, which is in N lat. 24° 45'. The Cacharis are a quiet industrious race, bearing more resemblance to the Chinese than to the Hindus; but they profess Hinduism. C. was wrested in 1824 from the Birmese, by whom it had been conquered in 1774, by a British force; and its rajah pays a tribute of 10,000 rupees to the East India company.
CACHEARI, a river of Brazil, in the Solimœus territory of Para, which rises under the meridian of 64° 10' W, and flows ESE to the Madeira, which it joins on the l. under 8° 40' S lat.
CACHENA. See CASHNA.
CACHETIA. See KARHETIA.
CACHIA. See SALEMBRIA. CAC
CACHICAMO, a small river of Venezuela, an affluent of the Orinoco on the I., in the prov. of Caraccas.

CACHIMAYO, a river of Bolivia, rising near Chayantes, and flowing in a SE direction past Chuquisaca, and through the prov. of Charcas to the Pilcomayo, which it joins on the I. bank near the meridian of 64° W, after a course of 300 m.
CACHIYACU, a river of Ecuador, which flows into the Huellaga, on the l. bank, 90 m. S of the confluence of the latter stream with the Amazon.

CACHOEIRA, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, on the Rio Paraguaçu, which flows through it, 15 m. above Maragogipe. It is a large and flourishing town, conducting an active commerce, especially in tobacco and cotton, and forming a principal entrepot to Bahia in communicating with the interior. Its pop., which in 1804 was only 1,088, now amounts to 15,000. It is liable to inundations from the river, which in December 1839 rose 20 ft. above its ordinary level, and submerged a large portion of the town.—Also a district and village in the prov. of Sao-Pedro-do-Rio-Grande, on the river Pardo.—Also a town in the prov. of Para, on the island of Marajo.—Also a small river in the prov. of Bahia, in the comarca and flowing into the bay of Ilheos .- Also a river in the prov. of Sao-Paulo, which flows into the bay of Paranagua, near Antonina.—The name is borne by a considerable number of hamlets and povoaçaos in Brazil; and, as signifying a cascade or fall, frequently occurs in the river-topography of that country.
CACONDA, a district and town in Benguela, on the N bank of the Cotape, 180 m. ESE of San Felipe.

CACONGO, or CACONGA, a state on the W coast of Africa, between Loango and the Zaire, which appears to be tributary to the king of Loango. Its principal port is Malemba, which is situated on the S side of the Cacongo river. It is a mountainous but fertile country.

CACORON, a village in the African state of Bambarra, between Loubakho and Sananso, containing between 500 and 600 inhabitants. The country in the neighbourhood is covered with the ce, or buttertree; and here and there exhibits granite blocks rising 6 or 7 ft. above the ground, and is generally composed of a very hard grey sand.—Caillie.
CACOVA. See KARAVA.

CACURI, a village of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Ultra, dist. and 23 m. NW of Cotrone, and 13 m. SW of Umbriatico, on a sandy mount. Pop. 1,000. It contains a fine collegiate and two parish churches, and a Dominican convent. In the environs is a salt mine.

CADALEN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Tarn, arrond. of Gaillac. The cant. comprises 7 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 6,374. The town is 6 m. SE of Gaillac, on the l. bank of the Candon. Pop. 2,190. It possesses a considerable commerce in cattle.
CADAMSTOWN, a parish in co. Kildare. Area 5,033 acres. Pop. 1,180. The Blackwater traces its boundary with co. Meath.
CADAQUES, a cape, town, and small port of Spain, in Catalonia, prov. and 30 m. NE of Gerona, and 6 m. E of Rosas.
CADAUJAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant of Labrede. Pop. 958.

CADAVAL, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Tras-os-Montes, on an affluent of the Douro, in the comarca and 15 m. E of Villa-Real.-Also a town in the prov. of Estremadura, in the comarca and 12 m. NE of Torres Vedras.
CADAVEDO, a town and small port of Spain, in Asturias, prov. and 30 m. WNW of Oviedo, at the entrance of a small river into the bay of Biscay.
CADBURY, a parish in Devonshire, 6 m. ENE of Crediton. Area 2,620 acres. Pop. 251.
CADBURY (North), a parish in Somersetshire, 3 m. S of Castlecary. Area 2,630 acres. Pop. 1,075.
CADBURY (South), a parish in Somersetshire, 6 m. WSW of Wincanton. Area 800 acres. Pop. 254.

CADDER, a parish in Lanarkshire, on the S side of the river Kelvin, and intersected by the Forth and Clyde canal, and the Glasgow and Garnkirk, and Edinburgh and Glasgow railways. Pop. in 1801, 2,120; in 1841, 4,425. Coal and lime are extensively wrought in this p., which contains the villages of Old and New Auchenairn, Bishopsbridge, and Chryston.
CADDINGTON, a parish, partly in Bedfordshire, partly in Herts, 2 m. SW by W of Luton. Area 4,500 acres, of which 1,600 are in Bedfordshire. Pop. 1,747, of whom 453 are in Bedfordshire.

CADDO, a parish in the NW of the state of Louisiana, U. S., between Red river on the E, and the Sabine river on the SW. It has Caddo lake in its N part, and Red river on its E boundary. Pop. 5,282. The cap. is Shreveport. The greater part of C. lake is quite shallow, and full of the trunks of decayed trees. It is connected with Red river by an outlet known as Caddo river, 40 m. in length, and partly navigable by steamboats.

CADEBY, a parish in Leicestershire, 2 m. ESE of Market-Bosworth. Area 2,130 acres. Pop. 387. -Also a township in the p. of Sprotsborough, W. R. of Yorkshire, 45 m. WSW of Doncaster. Area 1,110 acres. Pop. 1,153.
CADEMARIO, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Ticino, 7 m. WNW of Lugano, at the foot of a mountain of the same name. Pop. (Catholic) 244.
CADEN, a village of France, in the dep. of Morbihan. cant. and 5 m. S of Rochefort-en-Terre. Pop. 2,261.

CADENABBIA, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the district and 15 m. NNE of Como, on the W bank of the lake of that name, just above where it divides into two branches, 3 m. S of Menaggio, and nearly opposite to Bellagio. Eustace pronounces the view from this v. the most extensive and interesting on the lake.
CADENAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot, cant. of Figeac. Pop. 1,280.

CADENET, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, arrond. of Apt. The cant. comprises 9 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 10,699. The town is situated 11 m. S of Apt, in a fertile locality, at some distance from the r. bank of the Durance.
CADENIZZO, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Tessin, 4 m. SSW of Bellinzona.
CADEREITA, a town of Mexico, in the prov. and 16 m. WNW of Queretaro, and 110 m. NNW of Mexico. Pop. 3,000. In the environs are rich mines of silver, and porphyry-quarries.

CADER-IDRIS, a mountain 13 m. S of the town of Dolgelly, co. of Merioneth, the highest mountain in North Wales, Snowdon excepted. It rises almost perpendicularly from the margin of Lake Tallyllyn; and is on every side extremely steep and craggy. From its summit are seen Snowdon on the N; Plinlimmon on the S; the bay of Cardigan on the W; and on the E, the lake of Bala, the Arrenig, and the Arran mountains, with the long chain of hills reaching to the confines of Shropshire, including in clear days the Wrekin itself. Its component parts are silicious porphyry, quartz, and schorle. Colonel Mudge ascertained the alt. of this mountain to be 2,914 ft. CAD

CADEROUSSE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, cant. and 3 m. SW of Orange, on the r. bank of the Rhone. Pop. 3,169. It possesses manufactories of iron-ware, and silk spinningmills. Madder and silk are extensively grown in the environs.
CADIAC, a village of France, in the dep. of the Upper Pyrenees, cant. and 13 m. SW of Arreau. In the vicinity, on the bank of the Nesle, are two celebrated mineral springs.
CADIERE (LA), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Var, cant. of Le Beausset. Pop. 2,616. Coal has been wrought in the vicinity.

CADILLAC, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Gironde.—The cant., comprising 16 com., had a pop. of 11,767 in 1831.—The com. and town are on the r. bank of the Garonne, 20 m. SE of Bordeaux. Pop. 1,522. It is a place of considerable trade, and there is a large central house-ofcorrection for female prisoners here.
CADILLON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrenées, cant. of Lembeye. Pop. 357. Good red and white wines are grown here.
CADIMA, a village of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, in the comarca and 18 m. W of Coimbra, near the coast.
CADINANOS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 40 m. NNE of Burgos, on the r. bank of the Gerla.

CADIZ, a province or administrative division of Spain, in Andalusia; bounded on the N by the new prov. of Seville; on the E by that of Malaga; on the S by the straits of Gibraltar; and on the W by the Atlantic. Its length from N to S is about 60 m.; its breadth from E to W, 50 m. Its area is estimated at 216 Spanish sq. leagues. The principal rivers are the Guadalete and the Guadiaro. The Sierra de Ronda traverses its E quarter from N to S.—The pop. in 1833 was 324,703.—It is divided into 14 partidos: viz., 2 in the city of Cadiz, 2 in Jerez de la Frontera, and Algeciras, Arclos, Chiclana, Grazalema, Isla de Leon, Medinasidonia, Olvera, Puerto de Santa Maria, Sanlucar de Barrameda, and San Roque; which are subdivided into 44 pueblos. CADIZ, SPAN. Cadix; ITALIAN, Cadice; DUTCH, Kadix, a city of Spain, on the W coast of Andalusia, in N lat. 36° 31′ 53", W long. 6° 28′ 10", 45 m. NW of Gibraltar, and 60 m. SW of Seville, built on the extremity of a long tongue of land projecting in a NW direction from the Isla de Leon. The end of the tongue expands a little, and the whole of this expansion (C) is occupied by the city. It communicates with San Fernando (F), and the rest of the island, by a road (a a a) nearly 5 m. in length, running along a narrow sandy isthmus, which connects the point of land on which C. is built with the Isla. This isthmus is so narrow that the waves of the Atlantic on one side, and those of the bay of Cadiz on the other, reach the walls of the causeway. The San Pedro channel (b b b), an arm of the sea with a strong tide running through it, and communicating with both the bay and ocean, severs the Isla from the mainland. This channel is from 200 to 300 yards wide, deep, and muddy, and nowhere fordable at the lowest tide; and is crossed by a bridge of 5 arches, the Puente de San Pedro (d), protected by a double tête de pont. The town is surrounded by the sea on three of its sides; which circumstance, with the narrowness of the land-communication, prevents its capture by a military force, so long as the inhabitants or their allies are masters of the sea. This was strikingly exemplified in the long blockade of 1810, 1811, and 1812. C. is walled. On its sides washed by the sea, it has a lofty wall based on the rock, but much worn by the heavy waves of the Atlantic; on the land-front, a triple line of fortifications extends from sea to sea.—The ramparts have a circuit of 7,500 Spanish yards, and comprise 4 castles, viz., San Sebastian (c), Santa Catalina (e), the Muelle de San Felip (f), and the Muelle Principale (g). The highest part of it is about 200 ft. above the level of the sea. Its form is nearly a square of 12 m. on the side; and its population being considerable, there is little room to spare. The houses are lofty and well-built, in the Moorish style, with a palio or central court, and flat roofs, crowned by a mirador or turret. The streets are in some parts narrow, which, with the smallness of the windows, gives them a gloomy appearance, -a characteristic increased by the projecting slate-work in front of each house, which, however, is very conducive to coolness in hot weather. The streets are in general straight, clean, well paved and well lighted. The main street is upwards of 1 m. in length, wide, and rather handsome. The principal and only handsome square, the Plaza de San Antonio, is in the heart of the city. The chief buildings are the great hospital, the adecana or custom-house, the churches, the iglesia mayor or new cathedral-which is yet unfinished, though commenced in 1722—the jail, and two theatres. The private houses are in general white-washed and painted yellow. The light-house forms a conspicuous object on the W of the city; its lanthorn being elevated 148 ft. above the rocks on which it is built. The city contains 3,740 houses, 223 streets, 34 plazas or squares, many of them however little better than courts, 28 churches, 39 public edifices, and 5 gates. Nothing can surpass the beauty of "Fair Cadiz rising on the dark blue sea," when approaching the harbour, and when the eye takes in the numerous country-seats in the neighbourhood. "The strong deep light of a southern sky, reflecting from the lofty buildings of white freestone (?) which face the bay, rivets the eye of the navigator from the very verge of the horizon." [Doblado.] The bay of C., enclosed by the mainland on one side, and by the projecting tongue of land on the other, is one of the finest in the world, being from 10 to 12 leagues in circumference, with excellent anchoring ground, sheltered from the long swell of the Atlantic by sunken rocks, and having considerable protection from the neighbouring mountains. The entrance to this bay lies between the city and the promontory of Rota district, 54 m. NW by N of C. The Puntalenbaya, or inner harbour (P), is an extensive sheet of water when the tide is in; but the only channel for mooring ships is a narrow chasm in the mud between Forts Matagorda (h), and Punarsenal (A), on the N bank of the San Pedro, half-amile within its mouth, in which there formerly were 3 basins, and 12 building-slips: now, however, it is all but deserted. At the distance of 1,200 yards from the mouth of the San Pedro, or Rio de Sancto Petri, is a rocky islet bearing a fort which effectually commands the river, and is essential therefore to the defence of the city. At the mouth of the river there are extensive banks of shifting sands, which every year prove fatal to many. The bay of Cadiz is the grand rendezvous of the Spanish navy. The manufactures of Cadiz are insignificant, being confined to coarse linen, ribbons, and silk net-work; but its commercial industry is very great. It has long been the principal trading port in the south of Spain; and since 1720 it has been the seat of the board for American affairs, and of an East India company since 1732. The trade with the colonies of South America was an exclusive privilege of C., from 1720 until the opening of other ports in 1778, and subsequent years. This had some effect in lessening the share of C. in that trade, but it continued to engross the greater part of it until the South American colonies asserted their independence. In 1791—which may be quoted as an average year—the imports from South America amounted in gold and silver to £5,500,000 sterling; the number of vessels arriving from that quarter were 176; the total number of ships that entered the port was 1,010. The imports from America, exclusive of gold and silver, were cocoa, tobacco, sugar, chocolate, vanilla, and all kinds of colonial produce. The exports comprised hardware, linen and woollens, wine, oil, almonds, raisins, wax, cinnamon, paper, books, and medicines. The importations for 1837 from Havannah were 1,344,060 cwts. of sugar, 327,272 cwts. of coffee, 143,704,500 cigars, 1,196,185 lbs. of tobacco in leaf, 20,093 cwts. of treacle, 640 cwts. of honey, 8,221 cwts. of wax, and 314,622 gallons of rum. Hides, cochineal, and indigo, are also imported from South America; staves for wine-casks are imported from America; timber is imported from Russia, Sweden, and Norway; codfish is largely imported under the British flag; and silks, linens, woollens, iron hoops, tin, glass, hardware, earthenware, butter, and cheese, are imported, in Spanish vessels, from England, France, and Germany. The exportations consist chiefly of wines, quicksilver, salt, fruit, olive-oil, wool, and silk. The exportation of salt is at present about 2,520,000 quintals per ann. C. is still the great outlet for the foreign sherry trade. The annual average exports of wine from Xeres and Port-St.- Mary in 1835-6-7 was 28,627 butts of 600 bottles each, or 1,431,350 dozens. The exportation of sherry wines for the last 11 years has been as follows: Butts. Butts. C. has little European trade; the imposts upon European manufactures have reduced this branch to mere contraband. In a petition to the Congress from the provincial deputation in 1838, it is stated that "calamities, wars, loss of the Americas, and strangers profiting by its misfortunes, have banished opulence from C.;" and that, in consequence of the emigration of its inhabitants to other countries, one-third of the houses are vacant. In the adjoining country, particularly at Puerta de Santa Maria and Xeres de la Frontera, linen is manufactured in considerable quantity. The following tables of British legal commerce tales (i), and extending up to the Carracca, or grand with C. exhibit a gradual declension in the trade, attributable doubtless, in great part, to the present | 1740 it contained 5,000 families, 8 monasteries, and prohibitive system :- IMPORT TRADE. 1837 arrived 327 vessels, = 51.899 tons, value £60,948 The total number of vessels which entered the port in 1840 was 764, = 116,923 tons; of which 82, = 12,893 tons, were from Cuba, Porto-Rico, and the Philippine islands.

EXPORT TRADE. 1837 sailed 315 vessels, = <math>50,542</math> tons, value £834,133 The total number of vessels which cleared from the port in 1840 was <math>746 = 117,415</math> tons. In 1846, 3,693 Spanish vessels = 488,024 tons, entered the port, and 3,430 foreign vessels = 461,671 tons. The value of the total imports in 1840 was £1,076,600, of which £434,760 were from the Spanish foreign possessions; the exports amounted to £1,724,560, of which £251,320 were to the foreign possessions. Of the articles imported tobacco was the principal item, amounting to £148,320; linen ranked next, amounting to £129,200; leather, £111,680; cocoa, £102,000; sugar, £82,320. The principal article of export was wine, amounting to £1,314,880; provisions amounted to £112,920; oil to £35,800; silk to £29,840; salt to £22,760. The bank of C. has a nominal capital of 100,000,000 reals, or nearly £1,000,000; but somewhat less than only half of this capital has been emitted. The following statement of the assets and liabilities of this bank was published in July 1849:- ASSETS. Real, vel, Hard cash, . . . . Total, . ---- LIABILITIES. Capital disbursed, 16 per cent. of the shares, 7,949,120 Bank notes in circulation, . . . Total, . ACCOUNT OF THE SHARES MENTIONED IN THE ASSETS. Real. vel. Bank shares, . . The population of Cadiz, according to a census made on 1st December 1837, was 58,525, of whom 1,228 were outside of the walls. The number of casados or families was 8,611. It has at times exceeded this number, and at other times fallen below it, in consequence partly of the visitations of yellow fever, which raged here in 1800, 1804, 1810, and 1819, partly of political and commercial circumstances. In CADIZ. 1 nunnery.—The total number of deaths in C. in 38 years, from 1800 to 1837, was 110,345. The marriages for 10 years, from 1827 to 1836, were 1 in The public amusements are few. There is a tolerable theatre; but to enjoy the great national diversion, bull-fighting, the citizens must cross the bay to Puerto-Santa-Maria (M). The alameda, or public promenade, is a dusty gravel walk extending about half-a-mile along the ramparts. The inhabitants of C., being confined to the rock on which Cadiz is governed by a municipal council, called the ayuntamiento constitucional, which consists of the political chief as president, 4 alcaldes or judges, 16 regidores or magistrates, and 3 syndics. All the alcaldes, alternately 1 and 2 syndics, and half of the regidores, go out of office annually. Every bona fide inhabitant, 25 years old, has a right to vote for the councillors. When the elections are completed, the electors—who are double the number required— choose from amongst themselves the alcades and regidores. There are also various commissioners of police, public works, education, &c.; a tribunal of commerce, and a Philippine company. Justice is administered by 2 civil and criminal judges. The militia consisted in 1837 of 3 battalions of infantry within the walls, and a company without the walls; a company of light artillery, and the artillery of the fortress, and a regiment of marines. C. is also the see of a bishop, whose diocese, however, contains only 28 parishes. The convents of regulars, male and female, are suppressed. There are several public schools. In the college of San Bartolomeo instruction is given in theology, philosophy, and Latin. A college of medicine and surgery was founded in C. enjoys an equable climate. The average temp. at sunrise from June 1837 to May 1838 was 59° 40'; at noon, 68° 42'; at sunset, 64° 98'. The greatest height was in the months of July and August. In the former month, the therm at noon averaged 82°; in the latter, 83° 50'. The barometer averaged at sunrise, during these twelve months, 29.95; at noon, 29.98; at sunset, 29.95. One of the greatest inconbutts kept for the purpose, the annual expense of which is estimated at 100,000 piastres, or £20,000. Ice, which is used in great quantities, is brought from the mountains of Rondo. History.] The Phœnicians are supposed to have founded Cathe capture of Seville. After the discovery of America, it became, next to Seville, the richest city of Spain. This city was taken and pillaged in 1596 by the English, under Essex and Howard. In 1626, Lord Wimbledon was sent against C. with a fleet of 80 ships and an army of 10,000 men; but, after storming a fort, he was obliged to reimbark. In 1702 the Duke of Or mond and Sir George Rooke, in conjunction with the Dutch, made an attempt to seize Cadiz for the Archduke Charles; but after landing some troops, and summoning the fort of St. Catherine, they found it to be impracticable. C. was bombarded by the English in 1800. From its bay Villeneuve sailed in 1805 to fight the battle of Trafalgar. In 1808, the French fleet here surrendered to the Spaniards; and next year, when Seville fell into the hands of the French, C. became the seat first of the Central Junta, and afterwards of the Cortes. It now sustained a long blockade on the land-side from the French, which was not raised till after the battle of Salamanca, in 1812. In 1823, C. formed the stronghold of the constitutionalists until it was taken by the French, under the Duke D'Augouleme, who retained possession of it until the summer of 1828. Authorities.] Townsend's Travels in Spain.—Jacobs' Travels in the South of Spain.—Doblado's Letters in New Monthly Magazine, 1821 .- Scott's Ronda and Granada, 1838, 2 vols. 8vo.—United Service Journal. —Colonel Sykes in Statistical Journal, vol. i.—Espana Geografica Por Mellado. Madrid, 1845.

CADIZ, a township in the state of Ohio, U. S., cap. of Harrison co., 114 m. NE of Columbus. It is generally well cultivated, and contains a handsome and flourishing village. Pop. in 1840, 1,366; of v. 1,028.—Also a village of Trigg co., in the state of Kentucky, beautifully situated on Little river, 9 m. from Cumberland. Pop. 450. Its trade consists chiefly in tobacco.
CADLEY, or CADELEIGH, a parish of Devon, 41 m. SW of Tiverton. Area 2,020 acres. Pop. in 1841, 403.
